stepAnswer: Greater than or equal to $$121$$ because the majority of neighbors will always be benign $$=$$ $$1$$ since there are $$120$$ of them and at most $$60$$ benign $$=$$ $$0$$.
stepTitle: f) Suppose in our breast tumor training dataset we have $$60$$ benign $$=$$ $$0$$ data points and $$120$$ benign $$=$$ $$1$$ data points. For what values of k would we always predict the same class?
answerLatex: Greater than or equal to $$121$$ because the majority of neighbors will always be benign $$=$$ $$1$$ since there are $$120$$ of them and at most $$60$$ benign $$=$$ $$0$$.
choices: Greater than or equal to $$60$$ because once k exceeds the number of malignant points, they can never win the vote.
Greater than or equal to $$121$$ because the majority of neighbors will always be benign $$=$$ $$1$$ since there are $$120$$ of them and at most $$60$$ benign $$=$$ $$0$$.
Greater than $$180$$ because you'd need to include every single point before the prediction becomes constant.
Greater than or equal to $$1$$ because the classifier always predicts the same class since there are more benign $$=$$ $$1$$ points overall.
